summary: Paul urges intelligible, orderly worship in which spiritual gifts build up the whole church rather than elevate individuals.

outline.title: Gifts That Build Up
outline.summary: Paul prioritizes intelligible prophecy over uninterpreted tongues because gathered worship should strengthen the church.

outline.title: Speak So Others Understand
outline.summary: Spiritual speech benefits others only when it communicates meaning, so worship should engage both spirit and understanding.

outline.title: Mature Worship Before Outsiders
outline.summary: Orderly, intelligible prophecy can expose the heart and lead an unbelieving visitor to acknowledge God’s presence.

outline.title: Orderly Participation for Edification
outline.summary: Songs, teachings, revelations, tongues, and prophecies should be shared in turn under self-control for the church’s good.

outline.title: Decency and Order in Worship
outline.summary: Paul closes with instructions for submission, discernment, eagerness for prophecy, and orderly practice.

verses.bibleText: Follow after charity, and desire spiritual gifts, but rather that ye may prophesy.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The opening command grows directly out of the closing words of the previous chapter. Love is not one spiritual gift among many; it is the way in which every gift must be desired and exercised. Paul therefore tells the Corinthians to “pursue love.” The word suggests deliberate, persistent movement. Love is not merely a warm feeling that may appear during worship. It is the settled commitment to seek another person’s good, to refuse self-display, and to use whatever God gives for the strengthening of His people.

Pursuing love does not require indifference toward spiritual gifts. Paul also tells believers to desire them earnestly. The answer to disorder is not to suppress everything spiritual until worship becomes lifeless. God gives gifts to His church, and His people should welcome His work with gratitude. Yet desire itself must be purified. The question is not, “Which gift will make me appear most spiritual?” but, “Which gift will most clearly serve those gathered?”

That is why prophecy receives special emphasis. In this chapter prophecy is Spirit-given, intelligible speech that communicates truth to the congregation. It may bring instruction, conviction, encouragement, or comfort, but it is not exempt from evaluation. Paul will later require prophetic messages to be weighed. Its priority in public worship comes not from the greatness of the speaker but from its ability to be understood.

The Corinthians had been fascinated by visible expressions of spirituality. Paul redirects that eagerness without extinguishing it. Seek the Spirit’s gifts, he says, but pursue love first and through them all. Spiritual vitality and loving order are not enemies. The Spirit who distributes gifts is the Spirit who forms one body, and the gifts fulfill their purpose when they draw attention away from the gifted person and toward the truth, grace, and strengthening God intends for His church.

verses.bibleText: For he that speaketh in an unknown tongue speaketh not unto men, but unto God: for no man understandeth him; howbeit in the spirit he speaketh mysteries.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The King James Version supplies the word "unknown"; the Greek text simply says "a tongue." In the wider New Testament, tongues are meaningful human languages, as at Pentecost. Here the language is unknown to the congregation. Because no hearer understands it, the speech functions as address to God alone, who understands every language, rather than as communication to people.

The "mysteries" are not meaningless sounds or a secret prayer code. They are truths hidden from the listeners by a language barrier. Interpretation, that is, translation, can make the same content available to the church. Paul's concern is therefore not to protect unintelligibility but to overcome it. This reading fits the whole argument. A genuine gift can be misused when a speaker insists on a real human language that nobody present knows. The Spirit gives language for witness and service, not for display. In gathered worship, love requires speech that others can understand, test, and receive.

verses.bibleText: But he that prophesieth speaketh unto men to edification, and exhortation, and comfort.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Prophecy differs from an uninterpreted tongue because it speaks directly to people in language they can understand. Paul identifies three closely related effects: strengthening, encouragement, and comfort. These terms reveal the pastoral purpose of Spirit-given speech. Strengthening, or edification, pictures the church as something being built. Words spoken in worship should help believers become more firmly established in Christ, more grounded in truth, and more capable of faithful living. A message that draws admiration to the speaker while leaving the church spiritually unchanged has missed the purpose Paul values.

Comfort comes near to those who are burdened. God knows the grief, weakness, opposition, and uncertainty within His congregation. Through intelligible truth He steadies His people and reminds them that they are not abandoned. Prophecy therefore does not exist merely to predict unusual events or reveal hidden information. Its ordinary value lies in communicating God’s truth so that His people are helped.

These three effects also show why prophecy must be governed by love. Strengthening does not crush. Encouragement does not manipulate. Comfort does not flatter or conceal necessary truth. The Spirit’s work brings truth and grace together for the good of the hearer. Paul is not creating an absolute contrast in which tongues are bad and prophecy is good. He is comparing their effect when the church assembles. Prophecy arrives already intelligible; an uninterpreted tongue does not. The preference is therefore relational rather than competitive.

The most valuable public gift is not necessarily the most dramatic one. It is the one through which others can receive what God is giving. In a congregation tempted to measure spirituality by impressive experiences, Paul gives a different measure: Are people being strengthened? Are they being called courageously toward faithfulness? Are the wounded being comforted? Where those things occur through truthful, understandable speech, the Spirit is serving the body through love.

verses.bibleText: He that speaketh in an unknown tongue edifieth himself; but he that prophesieth edifieth the church.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
A person using a foreign language without translation may receive personal benefit from exercising the gift, but the congregation receives none. Paul states that limited result in order to contrast it with prophecy, which communicates intelligible truth and therefore strengthens the whole church.

Paul's point is not establishing a private ecstatic prayer language as a higher form of devotion. Throughout the chapter Paul keeps asking what spoken words mean and whether listeners can understand them. The tongue is a meaningful human language; the problem is that its meaning remains locked away from the people who need it.

Christian gifts are given for the common good. Personal benefit is never the governing purpose of public ministry. Love moves the gifted person away from self-contained experience and toward translation, teaching, and the edification of the body.

verses.bibleText: I would that ye all spake with tongues but rather that ye prophesied: for greater is he that prophesieth than he that speaketh with tongues, except he interpret, that the church may receive edifying.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul gladly recognizes the genuine gift of speaking human languages, especially valuable for carrying the gospel across linguistic boundaries. Yet in a congregation that does not know the language, prophecy is greater because it immediately communicates God's truth.

The comparison changes when the tongue is interpreted. Translation gives the foreign language the same public usefulness: the church can understand, evaluate, and be strengthened. The issue is not that one gift is more miraculous than another, but that intelligible ministry is more useful than untranslated speech. Paul's wish does not mean every believer must speak in tongues. Chapter 12 has already said that not all do. Nor is tongues the universal proof of receiving the Spirit. Paul desires every grace that advances mission, while insisting that gathered worship be governed by love and understanding.

verses.bibleText: Now, brethren, if I come unto you speaking with tongues, what shall I profit you, except I shall speak to you either by revelation, or by knowledge, or by prophesying, or by doctrine?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul makes the question personal. Even an apostle arriving with a genuine ability to speak other human languages would not help the Corinthians if he used a language they did not understand. Miraculous origin does not remove the need for communication. Revelation, knowledge, prophecy, and teaching all carry content. They disclose, explain, warn, encourage, and instruct. A foreign language can carry that same content only when the hearers know it or someone provides interpretation. Without translation, the gift remains real but its public use is fruitless.

This protects the church from judging a practice by spectacle. The proper question is not, "Was the experience impressive?" but, "Did Christ's people receive truth by it?" The Spirit's work is never threatened by clarity. He gives gifts so that the gospel can be understood.

verses.bibleText: And even things without life giving sound, whether pipe or harp, except they give a distinction in the sounds, how shall it be known what is piped or harped?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul turns to musical instruments. Even lifeless objects such as a flute or harp must produce distinct notes if anyone is to recognize the melody. Sound alone is not enough. Meaning depends upon distinction, pattern, and order.

The illustration does not demean music or emotion. Music can move the heart deeply precisely because its sounds are formed meaningfully. If every note collapses into undifferentiated noise, the instrument may still be loud, but it no longer communicates the intended tune.

The same principle applies to speech in worship. Spiritual intensity without intelligible form cannot convey truth to the congregation. A person may be sincere, but sincerity does not enable hearers to understand words they do not know. Paul’s appeal to “lifeless” instruments gently strengthens his point. If even an inanimate object must produce recognizable differences to serve its purpose, how much more should living believers use their God-given minds, voices, and gifts in a way that communicates?

Clarity does not require dullness. A flute does not become less musical because its notes are distinct. A harp is not less expressive because its strings are ordered. Likewise, worship does not become less spiritual when it is understandable. Form can carry life rather than extinguish it.

This protects the church from a false choice between spontaneity and meaning. Spontaneous music still needs recognizable notes, and spontaneous speech still needs interpretation or intelligible content. The Spirit’s freedom is not threatened by clarity. The illustration also reminds speakers that hearers cannot be blamed for failing to understand what has not been communicated. It is easy to interpret confusion as spiritual dullness in the audience. Paul places responsibility upon the one contributing publicly to make the sound distinct.

Every gift has a form appropriate to its purpose. Music requires distinguishable tones; instruction requires understandable words; tongues in the congregation require interpretation.

verses.bibleText: For if the trumpet give an uncertain sound, who shall prepare himself to the battle?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
A trumpet used to signal action must give a clear call. If the sound is uncertain, those who hear it will not know how to prepare. The failure is not merely aesthetic; it has practical consequences.

Paul’s image emphasizes that communication can guide conduct only when its meaning is recognizable. An indistinct signal may create hesitation, confusion, or a completely wrong response. The hearers cannot be expected to act wisely when they do not know what they are being told. Public worship also prepares God’s people for faithful living. Instruction may call them to repentance, courage, service, endurance, or hope. If the message remains unintelligible, it cannot equip them to respond.

The trumpet illustration does not suggest that worship should become militaristic or emotionally cold. It highlights purpose. A signal exists for those who hear it. Its usefulness is measured by whether they can recognize and act upon it.

Some Corinthians may have valued the occurrence of a spiritual manifestation without asking what it accomplished. Paul insists that a gift is not fully serving its public purpose merely because it has been exercised. The church must receive a clear call. This principle applies equally to teaching, prayer, testimony, and song. Words may be numerous yet leave people uncertain about the truth being proclaimed. Emotional force may be strong while direction remains unclear. Love does not confuse intensity with usefulness.

The trumpet also reminds leaders that confusion can leave people unprepared. Vague warnings, undefined expectations, and unexplained spiritual claims may burden a congregation without guiding it. God’s people deserve communication that helps them know how to respond faithfully.

Paul’s larger concern remains edification. Worship should strengthen the church for life under Christ’s lordship. The trumpet must sound distinctly because the people must be prepared. Likewise, spiritual speech should convey recognizable truth so that hearers can believe, obey, endure, and move together in the direction God is calling them.

verses.bibleText: So likewise ye, except ye utter by the tongue words easy to be understood, how shall it be known what is spoken? for ye shall speak into the air.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Speech must contain recognizable words if it is to communicate. A real human language is meaningful to those who know it, but to hearers who do not, it may sound like speech sent into empty air. The defect is not in the language; it is in using it without regard for the audience. Paul's rule applies to preaching, prayer, singing, teaching, and testimony. Correct words that cannot be understood in the setting do not fulfill public ministry. Translation is therefore an act of love, not a concession to unbelief.

The church should never equate obscurity with spirituality. God may transcend human understanding, but He addresses people in language and calls the mind to respond. Worship honors Him when truth is spoken faithfully and received intelligently.

verses.bibleText: There are, it may be, so many kinds of voices in the world, and none of them is without signification.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul appeals to the ordinary world of languages. Human communities use many voices, and each language has real meaning for those who know it. This analogy confirms that the tongues under discussion are not meaningless sounds. They are languages whose significance is lost when speaker and hearer do not share the code.

Corinth was a mobile Roman port city where travelers, merchants, migrants, and visitors brought many languages into contact. A multilingual setting made both the missionary usefulness of tongues and the confusion of untranslated speech easy to imagine.

The Spirit's gift does not cancel the structure of language. It enables communication that otherwise would not be possible. In the church, that purpose is honored when the language is understood directly or faithfully translated.

verses.bibleText: Therefore if I know not the meaning of the voice, I shall be unto him that speaketh a barbarian, and he that speaketh shall be a barbarian unto me.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
When speaker and hearer do not understand one another, each experiences the other as a foreigner. The Greek term can describe someone whose speech sounds unintelligible because the language is not shared. Paul is describing a normal linguistic barrier, not a heavenly code.

The gospel creates one body across nations, but unity does not make language differences disappear automatically. Translation, patience, and willingness to listen are practical forms of Christian hospitality. Refusing them can turn a genuine gift into a wall. Public worship should make strangers able to hear the truth rather than making fellow believers feel like outsiders. The Spirit overcomes barriers for mission; pride rebuilds them for display.

verses.bibleText: Even so ye, forasmuch as ye are zealous of spiritual gifts, seek that ye may excel to the edifying of the church.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul acknowledges the Corinthians’ eagerness for spiritual manifestations. He does not condemn their desire for the Spirit’s work. He redirects it: since they are eager, they should seek to abound in what builds up the church. The command exposes the difference between spiritual enthusiasm and spiritual maturity. Enthusiasm asks for more experience. Maturity asks how that experience can become a blessing to others.

“To abound” suggests generous fullness. Paul does not want the Corinthians to become cautious to the point of spiritual inactivity. He wants their gifts, prayers, songs, teachings, and prophetic words to overflow—but in a direction that strengthens the body.

Edification remains the governing standard. This does not mean every contribution must please everyone or avoid all correction. A truthful rebuke may build up by calling people away from sin. The issue is whether the contribution serves Christ’s constructive purpose for His church. Paul also corrects competition. If the goal is personal prominence, gifts become instruments for comparison. If the goal is the church’s growth, believers can rejoice when another person’s gift serves more effectively.

Spiritual abundance is therefore measured differently from visible activity. A meeting may contain many voices and still provide little edification. Another may include fewer contributions, yet each one communicates truth, encourages faith, and invites thoughtful response.

Paul’s language does not place responsibility only upon leaders. The whole congregation is addressed. Everyone who contributes should consider the body. Those who speak, sing, pray, interpret, evaluate, and listen share responsibility for the health of worship. The church does not need less of the Spirit. It needs spiritual life governed by the Spirit’s own purpose. The One who gives gifts also creates unity, self-control, truth, and love.

Eagerness becomes beautiful when it is freed from self-display. Seek to abound, Paul says, but abound in what helps the church become more faithful to Christ. That is spiritual fullness worthy of desire.

verses.bibleText: Wherefore let him that speaketh in an unknown tongue pray that he may interpret.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The speaker in a tongue should pray for interpretation: the ability, or the provision through another person, to translate the meaningful human language for the congregation. Paul does not tell the church to become comfortable with unexplained speech. He tells the gifted person to seek the companion grace that makes communication possible.

Prayer acknowledges dependence. A genuine language gift remains under the Spirit's purpose and the church's need. The speaker cannot claim that divine origin exempts the message from being understood and tested.

Interpretation turns a private linguistic capacity into public ministry. Once translated, the church can affirm the truth, learn from it, and respond together. Love therefore prays not merely to speak, but to be understood.

verses.bibleText: For if I pray in an unknown tongue, my spirit prayeth, but my understanding is unfruitful.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul considers prayer offered in a language not understood in the gathering. The spirit may be genuinely engaged, yet the mind bears no fruit in the shared life of the church because the content is inaccessible. Whether the speaker follows every word of the Spirit-given language or not, the public problem is the same: others cannot understand.

This is not praise for a private ecstatic prayer language. Paul immediately resolves the problem by requiring spirit and understanding together. A meaningful human language must be translated if it is used aloud among people who do not know it. Christian prayer is more than vocal intensity. It engages truth, intention, faith, and the mind. The Spirit does not bypass understanding as though the mind were an enemy; He renews it and makes worship fruitful for the whole person and the whole church.

verses.bibleText: What is it then? I will pray with the spirit, and I will pray with the understanding also: I will sing with the spirit, and I will sing with the understanding also.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul's conclusion is integration: pray with the spirit and with understanding, sing with the spirit and with understanding. He refuses a choice between spiritual vitality and an engaged mind. If a song or prayer is offered in another human language, the congregation needs interpretation so that worshipers can understand and join it. The goal is not merely that the speaker feels devotion, but that the body can participate truthfully.

This principle guards against both cold formalism and uncontrolled enthusiasm. Christian worship should be heartfelt, Spirit-led, doctrinally sound, and intelligible. The same Spirit who awakens praise also enables God's people to know what they are saying.

verses.bibleText: Else when thou shalt bless with the spirit, how shall he that occupieth the room of the unlearned say Amen at thy giving of thanks, seeing he understandeth not what thou sayest?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The listener cannot say "Amen" responsibly to words he does not understand. In biblical worship, Amen is not a reflexive sound; it is the congregation's informed agreement that what has been said is true and offered to God.

A blessing in a foreign human language may be sincere and beautiful, but without translation it excludes the hearer. Paul evaluates the practice from the position of the person without the language, not from the excitement of the speaker.

Corporate worship is participatory. Interpretation enables the church to consent, give thanks, and stand together before God. Any practice that repeatedly prevents informed participation has missed the Spirit's purpose for the assembly.

verses.bibleText: For thou verily givest thanks well, but the other is not edified.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul concedes that the thanksgiving itself may be genuine. The failure lies in its untranslated public form: the other person is not built up because he cannot understand it.

This distinction is important. A real gift of human language can carry true praise and still be used unwisely. Sincerity does not make every expression appropriate for gathered worship. Love asks what the hearer receives. The solution is not to forbid gratitude or language gifts. It is to translate them or to remain silent publicly. The Spirit's gifts reach their intended goal when another believer can share the truth and be strengthened.

verses.bibleText: I thank my God, I speak with tongues more than ye all:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul's own experience prevents anyone from treating him as hostile to tongues. His travels and mission placed him among many peoples, and he could thank God for abundant ability to speak languages useful to the gospel. The verse gives no basis for boasting in a private ecstatic practice. Paul thanks God for the gift and then immediately limits its public use in Corinth. A gift is valuable in the setting where it communicates; the same gift can be unhelpful when no listener knows the language.

Paul's gratitude and restraint belong together. He neither suppresses a genuine gift nor makes it a badge of rank. He uses what God gives under the law of love and the demands of mission.

verses.bibleText: Yet in the church I had rather speak five words with my understanding, that by my voice I might teach others also, than ten thousand words in an unknown tongue.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The contrast is intentionally extreme: five intelligible words can teach, while ten thousand untranslated words cannot. Paul values communication over volume, duration, and spectacle.

The "unknown tongue" is a human language unknown to the local hearers. It may be perfectly meaningful elsewhere, but in this church it requires interpretation. Without translation, multiplying words only multiplies exclusion.

Teaching is central because the gathered church must learn the truth, test what is said, and grow in Christ. The verse therefore supplies a lasting rule for worship: clarity in a few faithful words is more spiritual than impressive speech that leaves the congregation uninstructed.

verses.bibleText: Brethren, be not children in understanding: howbeit in malice be ye children, but in understanding be men.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul addresses the Corinthians affectionately as brothers and sisters, yet he speaks firmly: they must stop being children in their thinking. Their fascination with impressive manifestations had not proved maturity. It had revealed a failure to think about love, edification, and the effect of worship upon others.

He does not condemn every childlike quality. He tells them to be infants regarding evil. Innocence toward wickedness is desirable. Believers should not become sophisticated in manipulation, rivalry, impurity, or the misuse of spiritual power. In thinking, however, they must become mature. Mature judgment weighs more than immediate excitement. It asks what honors God, serves the body, communicates truth, and helps outsiders understand.

Maturity also includes the ability to receive correction. Children may insist upon immediate desire; mature believers allow love and truth to limit them. They can refrain from speaking, wait their turn, seek interpretation, and submit their contribution to evaluation.

The verse prepares for Paul’s scriptural quotation. The Corinthians need mature understanding of tongues as a sign. What they regarded as unquestionable evidence of spiritual blessing could, under certain conditions, resemble a sign of judgment. Paul calls them to grow up without losing purity. Their minds should become capable of understanding the consequences of their choices, while their hearts remain unpracticed in evil.

The Spirit’s work does not make believers less thoughtful. He forms people whose minds are renewed and whose gifts serve wisely. Mature worship is spiritually alive, morally innocent, and deeply attentive to the good of the whole congregation.

verses.bibleText: In the law it is written, With men of other tongues and other lips will I speak unto this people; and yet for all that will they not hear me, saith the Lord.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul quotes the warning in Isaiah about God addressing His people through the foreign speech of invading nations. The "other tongues" are plainly human languages, and their unintelligibility functions as a sign of judgment upon persistent unbelief. This Old Testament setting helps define Paul's use of tongues. A language barrier can signal distance rather than blessing when hearers refuse God's clear word. The miraculous gift is not automatically saving; response to the truth remains necessary.

The church should therefore seek the missionary use of languages that makes the gospel clear, not reproduce the confusion of judgment inside worship. Interpretation changes foreign speech from a barrier into an intelligible witness.

verses.bibleText: Wherefore tongues are for a sign, not to them that believe, but to them that believe not: but prophesying serveth not for them that believe not, but for them which believe.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Tongues can serve as a sign to unbelievers when the Spirit crosses a real human-language barrier, as at Pentecost, or as a warning when foreign speech exposes unbelief, as in Isaiah. The sign is not ecstatic sound offered as proof that a speaker is spiritually superior.

Prophecy ordinarily serves believers through intelligible strengthening, yet verses 24-25 show that it can also convict an unbeliever. Paul is not drawing an absolute wall between audiences. He is contrasting the normal effects of untranslated language and understandable truth.

A sign reaches its proper goal only when it points beyond the speaker to God's message. The church should never turn tongues into a performance that obscures the gospel the gift was meant to carry.

verses.bibleText: If therefore the whole church be come together into one place, and all speak with tongues, and there come in those that are unlearned, or unbelievers, will they not say that ye are mad?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
If many people speak different human languages at once and no one translates, a visitor hears only overlapping sounds. The likely conclusion is not that the Spirit is present but that the congregation has lost control.

This scenario exposes the failure of using genuine gifts without love. The languages may be real, and the speakers may be sincere, yet simultaneity and lack of interpretation destroy communication. Paul cares about outsiders because worship bears public witness. Order is evangelistic. When the church speaks one at a time and in language people understand, visitors can encounter truth rather than confusion.

verses.bibleText: But if all prophesy, and there come in one that believeth not, or one unlearned, he is convinced of all, he is judged of all:

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The picture changes when the congregation speaks prophetically in an orderly and intelligible way. If an unbeliever or uninformed person enters, he may be convicted by all and called to account by all. “All” should be understood within the ordered participation Paul will soon regulate. The prophets do not speak simultaneously. Their messages come one by one, allowing truth to reach the hearer clearly.

Conviction is not created by public cruelty. Prophecy does not exist to expose a visitor for entertainment or to humiliate someone with private information. The intelligible word brings the person under the searching light of God’s truth.

To be “called to account” means that the hearer recognizes moral and spiritual responsibility before God. Sin, motives, loyalties, and need become visible in the light of the gospel. The phrase “by all” suggests the cumulative witness of the gathered church. Different messages may converge upon the same divine truth, and the life of the community supports what is spoken. God uses the body together.

This does not mean every prophetic statement is automatically correct. Verse 29 requires evaluation. Conviction arises through truth that has been faithfully communicated and responsibly discerned.

The unbeliever’s response also shows that prophecy’s stated orientation toward believers in verse 22 is not exclusive. What edifies the church may also awaken the outsider. The church does not need to abandon worship in order to become evangelistic. When worship is intelligible, truthful, holy, and loving, the visitor can encounter the reality believers confess.

Paul’s vision is not of a polished performance designed to manipulate emotions. It is a community in which God’s word is spoken clearly enough to address conscience.

The contrast with verse 23 is profound. Uncontrolled tongues leave the visitor judging the church. Intelligible prophecy places the visitor beneath the judgment of God’s truth.

verses.bibleText: And thus are the secrets of his heart made manifest; and so falling down on his face he will worship God, and report that God is in you of a truth.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
As intelligible prophetic truth reaches the visitor, “the secrets of the heart” are disclosed. The person falls down, worships God, and declares that God is truly among the congregation.

The secrets are not necessarily unknown facts announced publicly by a speaker. God’s truth exposes motives, hidden sins, fears, and loyalties by revealing the heart to itself before Him. A person may hear no private detail mentioned and yet recognize, “God knows me, and His word has addressed my real condition.”

Prophecy should therefore not be turned into spiritual surveillance or public entertainment. Humiliating people, guessing private information, or displaying supposed supernatural knowledge is far from Paul’s goal. The desired end is repentance and worship. Falling down expresses surrender and reverence. The visitor’s attention is no longer fixed upon the gifted speakers. He recognizes God’s presence.

The declaration “God is really among you” reveals the true sign of spiritual worship. It is not the sheer number of manifestations, the volume of the gathering, or the unusual nature of the experience. God’s presence becomes recognizable when His truth reaches the conscience and leads people to bow before Him.

Conviction and comfort are not opposites. The same truth that uncovers sin can reveal grace. A heart exposed before God may discover that He calls sinners not merely to shame but to repentance and restoration. The whole congregation shares responsibility for creating this environment. Order, evaluation, intelligibility, and love allow the word to be heard.

The contrast with chaotic tongues is not a contrast between spiritual and nonspiritual worship. Both gifts may come from the Spirit. The difference is whether the gathering makes God’s truth accessible.

When worship is faithful, the outsider may move from observation to confession, from hiddenness to honesty, and from distance to worship. The church is built up, and God—not the gifted individual—is recognized as present among His people.

verses.bibleText: How is it then, brethren? when ye come together, every one of you hath a psalm, hath a doctrine, hath a tongue, hath a revelation, hath an interpretation. Let all things be done unto edifying.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul pictures broad participation: a song, teaching, revelation, human language, or interpretation may be offered. Diversity is welcome, but every contribution submits to one purpose: building the church.

Tongue and interpretation belong together because a foreign language must be translated. Revelation and prophecy are likewise subject to evaluation. No participant may claim private inspiration as permission to ignore the body. This verse is not a script for competitive spontaneity. It is a framework of responsible participation in which gifts remain under self-control, Scripture, and love. The question for each contribution is whether Christ's people can understand and be strengthened.

verses.bibleText: If any man speak in an unknown tongue, let it be by two, or at the most by three, and that by course; and let one interpret.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul gives precise limits for public use of a meaningful human language: only two or at most three speakers, one at a time, and an interpreter must translate. These rules make little sense if the goal were uncontrolled ecstatic expression. They fit a language gift intended to communicate. "One interpret" may mean one qualified translator for the sequence or, at minimum, that interpretation must be provided. Either way, the church is not asked to accept speech it cannot understand.

The restriction protects time, order, and equality. A genuine gift remains under the speaker's self-control and the congregation's need. Divine origin is shown not by disorder but by willing obedience to the purpose of edification.

verses.bibleText: But if there be no interpreter, let him keep silence in the church; and let him speak to himself, and to God.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
If no interpreter is present, the language speaker must remain silent in the church. The command is categorical. A meaningful human language that cannot be translated has no public ministry in that gathering.

"Speak to himself and to God" does not establish a private ecstatic prayer code. It directs the person away from audible congregational speech: he may quietly hold the matter before God, who understands every language, but he must not occupy the assembly with words others cannot receive.

The same chapter requires situational silence from prophets when another receives a revelation and from disruptive questioners later in the passage. Silence is therefore not rejection of the person or gift. It is self-controlled love that preserves intelligible worship.

verses.bibleText: Let the prophets speak two or three, and let the other judge.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Prophets, like speakers in other languages, must yield the floor. Paul limits the number to two or three and then requires “the other” or “the others” to weigh what has been said. Intelligible speech is not automatically trustworthy speech; a claim to inspiration never exempts its content from examination.

The verb translated “judge” carries the sense of discerning or evaluating. The hearers ask whether the message accords with the apostolic gospel, the character of God, and the Spirit’s purpose of building up the congregation. This is neither cynical suspicion nor automatic acceptance. The church listens openly, retains what is true, and refuses what is false or misapplied.

Such weighing distinguishes congregational prophecy from the canonical authority of Scripture. A prophet in Corinth could bring a timely word, yet the message remained accountable to the revelation already entrusted to the church. “God told me” could not be used to silence questions, control another person, or outrank the gospel.

The command also checks celebrity. A gifted speaker is one member of the body, not an unanswerable spiritual ruler. Evaluation should therefore be serious without becoming humiliating; its purpose is the safety and edification of the whole assembly.

Paul’s order leaves room for genuine spiritual gifts while protecting the church from manipulation. Openness to the Spirit and testing by the Word are partners. Where either is missing, worship becomes vulnerable—either to a cold refusal of God’s work or to confident claims that no one is permitted to examine.

wordNotes.explanation: To discern, distinguish, or evaluate. Prophetic speech is to be heard seriously and tested rather than accepted merely because a speaker claims inspiration.

verses.bibleText: If any thing be revealed to another that sitteth by, let the first hold his peace.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
If a revelation comes to another person who is seated, the first speaker should become silent. Paul again requires yielding. No prophet has the right to continue indefinitely simply because he began first. The seated person’s revelation does not justify interrupting chaotically. The first speaker must recognize the moment and make room. The transition remains orderly.

This instruction assumes that prophets retain awareness of what is happening around them. Inspiration does not place them in an uncontrollable state. They can notice, pause, and yield.

Silence becomes an act of service. The first prophet does not lose dignity by stopping. He demonstrates that the gathering belongs to Christ rather than to the speaker. The rule also prevents monopolizing spiritual authority. A gifted person may be tempted to assume that everything he has to say must be heard immediately and fully. Paul insists that others may also receive something valuable.

The revelation itself will still need to be weighed. The fact that it arises during another contribution does not make it automatically superior. Paul is regulating sequence, not ranking messages.

This arrangement requires mutual trust. The first speaker trusts that his value does not depend upon holding the floor. The second waits for space rather than forcing it. The congregation listens and discerns. In many churches, disorder comes not from a lack of gifts but from unwillingness to yield. People may speak over one another, defend their time, or interpret interruption as divine urgency. Paul associates the Spirit with patient self-restraint.

The willingness to become silent is one of the clearest signs that a speaker seeks edification rather than prominence. Love can stop. It can listen. It can rejoice that God may use another member.

Orderly yielding allows the church to receive a fuller range of grace. No voice needs to crush another, and no gift needs to prove itself through domination. The Spirit’s abundance becomes visible through shared participation shaped by humility.

verses.bibleText: For ye may all prophesy one by one, that all may learn, and all may be comforted.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul says that all may prophesy one by one so that everyone may learn and everyone may be encouraged. The emphasis falls upon possibility within order, not simultaneous speech.

“All” does not require that every member prophesy during every gathering. It opens participation beyond a single dominant voice. Those whom God enables may contribute in turn.

“One by one” protects the purposes named next. Learning requires listening, and encouragement requires receiving. Neither can occur when voices overlap. Prophecy is again described by its effect rather than its excitement. People learn truth and are strengthened to live faithfully. A message that creates confusion but no learning has not fulfilled this purpose.

The mutuality is important. The church is not divided permanently into a small class that gives and a large class that only receives. Different members may serve at different times, and all remain learners under God.

This does not remove recognized leadership or responsibility. The messages are still limited and evaluated. Participation and accountability remain inseparable. “Everyone may learn” implies that understandable content matters. The Spirit teaches through words that enter the mind. Learning is not opposed to encounter with God; it is one way He transforms His people.

“Everyone may be encouraged” reminds speakers that truth should be applied pastorally. Even correction should point toward faithful response and hope.

Without order, the loudest or most assertive dominate. With orderly turns, gifts that might otherwise be overlooked can serve the body. The congregation becomes both a school and a community of encouragement. Believers receive insight, discern together, and strengthen one another under the Spirit’s guidance.

This participatory vision requires patience. A person may wait, shorten a contribution, or defer to another. Such restraint is not loss; it makes shared learning possible.

verses.bibleText: And the spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“The spirits of prophets are subject to prophets.” Paul states one of the chapter’s most important principles. Genuine spiritual inspiration does not eliminate self-control.

The “spirits” may refer to the inward prophetic impulse or the spiritual activity associated with the message. Whatever the precise nuance, the prophet remains capable of governing when and how he speaks. No one may excuse disorder by saying, “I could not help myself; the Spirit forced me.” The Holy Spirit does not contradict the order, love, and peace He produces.

It also protects prophets from the burden of thinking every inward impression must be voiced immediately. They may wait, discern, pray, and submit the matter to the community.

Self-control is not spiritual coldness. It is part of the Spirit’s mature work. Deep emotion and strong conviction may coexist with patience and order. The statement follows the command to yield to another speaker. A prophet can stop because the gift remains under responsible control.

The principle also supports evaluation. If the prophet remains accountable for the message, he may listen when others weigh it. He need not defend every word as beyond examination.

The Spirit’s freedom and human self-control are not rivals. The Spirit frees believers from the need to dominate, interrupt, and display themselves. He enables them to speak courageously and to become silent lovingly. A prophet most clearly honors God not by appearing overwhelmed beyond responsibility, but by using the gift in a way that reflects truth, humility, discernment, and concern for the body. Inspiration becomes credible when it bears the character of the God who gives it.

verses.bibleText: For God is not the author of confusion, but of peace, as in all churches of the saints.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
God is not a God of disorder or confusion but of peace. Paul grounds his practical regulations in the character of God. Worship should reflect the One being worshipped. The disorder Paul addresses is not every unfamiliar, emotional, or culturally different expression. It is the confusion created when people speak simultaneously, without interpretation, without yielding, and without evaluation. Such disorder prevents understanding and edification.

Peace is more than quietness. A silent gathering may still be controlled, fearful, or spiritually lifeless. Biblical peace is the harmonious order in which truth can be heard, gifts can serve, and people can participate without rivalry.

God’s peace does not suppress diversity. Chapter 12 has celebrated many gifts and members. Peace allows those differences to work together rather than compete. The statement also challenges the idea that chaos proves divine freedom. God may surprise His people, stir deep emotion, and speak through unexpected members, but He does not need confusion to reveal His presence.

The phrase concerning “all the churches of the saints” reminds Corinth that these principles are not private standards invented for one group alone. The congregation belongs to a wider people whose worship is also shaped by peace and responsible order.

This wider reference does not mean every church must adopt identical cultural forms. It means the Corinthians cannot appeal to local enthusiasm as an excuse to reject the common apostolic concern for edification. God’s character provides both comfort and correction. He is not the author of the anxious competition that leaves people unheard and confused. He draws His church toward reconciliation, attentiveness, and mutual service.

Peace may require firm limits. Telling someone to wait, seek interpretation, or submit a message to evaluation is not necessarily unloving. Boundaries can protect the fellowship in which gifts flourish.

verses.bibleText: Let your women keep silence in the churches: for it is not permitted unto them to speak; but they are commanded to be under obedience as also saith the law.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Let your women keep silence” cannot mean that every woman must remain mute throughout Christian worship. Paul has already regulated women who pray and prophesy in the assembly (11:5), and the present chapter tells several groups to be silent in particular circumstances: a language-speaker without an interpreter (14:28), a prophet when another receives a revelation (14:30), and now these women. The silence is situational, not an erasure of every form of speech.

The immediate clue comes in verse 35, where the women are told to ask questions at home. One likely setting is the weighing of prophecies in verses 29–33. Wives may have been interrupting that process with questions, perhaps even publicly challenging their husbands in ways that carried shame in Corinthian society. Another view takes the command more broadly as a restraint on disruptive speech. The wording does not settle every detail, so the interpretation should remain proportionate to the evidence.

“As also saith the law” probably appeals to the scriptural pattern of ordered relations rather than to a command that women never speak in worship; no such blanket command appears in the Old Testament. Submission here belongs to Paul’s larger concern that gifted people govern their speech for the peace and instruction of the body.

This passage must not be isolated from the women who prayed, prophesied, labored in the gospel, and served the churches. Nor can two difficult verses by themselves settle every modern question about ordination or church office. Paul is correcting disorder in Corinthian worship. His remedy protects intelligibility and honor without revoking the Spirit’s gifts to women.

verses.bibleText: And if they will learn any thing, let them ask their husbands at home: for it is a shame for women to speak in the church.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
The reference to asking “their husbands at home” narrows the problem. Paul appears to address married women whose questions were disrupting the assembly, not women as an undifferentiated class. In a setting where education varied sharply and wives might sit apart from husbands, an earnest question could still create disorder if it repeatedly interrupted public instruction or the evaluation of prophecy.

Paul’s answer is practical: let the discussion continue at home rather than taking over the gathering. That direction assumes husbands who are willing and able to speak with their wives; it cannot become an excuse to deny women access to biblical instruction. The goal is that the congregation learn in an orderly way, not that women remain uninformed.

Calling the disruptive speech “a shame” reflects the public honor concerns already visible in chapter 11. Conduct that publicly embarrassed a spouse or confused worship could bring reproach on the gospel. The enduring issue is not the cultural arrangement of seating or education but the loving discipline by which every worshiper yields personal speech to the good of the body.

Verse 35 must be read with 11:5 and with the repeated, temporary silences of 14:28 and 14:30. Women may pray and prophesy; prophets and language-speakers may also have to stop speaking. No gift creates a right to interrupt. These lines therefore provide rules for ordered participation, not a universal ban on women teaching, praying, testifying, or using spiritual gifts. They also do not resolve the separate debate about ordination, which requires attention to the whole witness of Scripture rather than a disputed reconstruction of one Corinthian disturbance.

verses.bibleText: What? came the word of God out from you? or came it unto you only?

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul confronts Corinthian pride with two pointed questions: Did the word of God originate with them? Did it reach only them? The answer to both is no. The Corinthians were recipients of the gospel, not its creators. They could not reshape worship according to local preference while disregarding the apostolic message through which they came to faith.

Nor were they the only congregation Christ had gathered. They belonged to a wider fellowship of churches. Spiritual giftedness did not make them independent from the rest of God’s people.

The questions expose a form of congregational arrogance. A church may become so impressed with its own experiences that it assumes its practices are beyond correction. Paul reminds them that God’s word existed before their local innovations and had borne fruit far beyond their city. This appeal to wider practice does not mean tradition is always right or that majority custom can replace Scripture. Paul’s authority rests upon the word of God and the gospel of Christ, not mere institutional uniformity.

The Corinthians had perhaps treated their disorderly worship as evidence of exceptional spiritual freedom. Paul asks whether they imagine themselves to be the source or exclusive destination of God’s revelation.

The questions also protect the church from personalities who claim unique revelation unavailable to everyone else. God may give gifts locally, but He does not place one congregation above accountability to His revealed word. Paul does not suppress Corinth’s gifts. He places them within the communion of the saints. Their hymns, prophecies, tongues, and teachings must serve the same Christ confessed throughout the churches.

The word of God did not begin with them, and it had not stopped with them. They were one beloved part of a much greater work.

True spiritual maturity receives this gladly. It does not need to be unique in order to be valued. It can accept correction, submit innovation to truth, and rejoice that God is building a people larger than any one congregation’s experience.

verses.bibleText: If any man think himself to be a prophet, or spiritual, let him acknowledge that the things that I write unto you are the commandments of the Lord.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Anyone who considers himself a prophet or spiritually gifted should recognize that Paul’s instructions are the Lord’s command. The claim to spirituality is tested by willingness to submit to apostolic truth.

The Corinthians may have assumed that powerful experiences gave them authority to disregard Paul’s regulations. He reverses the test. The genuinely spiritual person will discern the divine authority within the instruction.

Paul is not defending every personal preference as though it were a command from God. He speaks as an apostle entrusted with the gospel and commissioned to order the churches under Christ’s lordship. The command concerns the use of gifts in love, intelligibility, evaluation, self-control, and peace. These are not arbitrary restrictions designed to increase Paul’s control. They express the Lord’s concern for His body.

Indeed, the chapter itself opposes unaccountable control. Prophecy must be weighed. Speakers must yield. No gift holder may dominate. Apostolic authority creates accountability rather than removing it.

The person claiming to be prophetic should therefore welcome discernment and order. Refusal to submit may reveal that the claim is driven by pride rather than the Spirit. Recognition involves more than intellectual agreement. The Corinthians must alter their worship accordingly. Acknowledging the command means limiting speakers, requiring interpretation, weighing prophecy, and pursuing peace.

True spirituality is not measured by how often someone claims divine revelation. It is seen in humble responsiveness to what God has already made known.

Paul’s challenge reaches the conscience: spiritual gifts do not place anyone above the word. The Spirit who gives prophecy is the Spirit who inspired apostolic instruction. He does not lead people to despise the order He has commanded. The mature prophet recognizes the Lord’s voice not only in the moment of inspiration but also in the boundaries that protect the church. Submission to those boundaries is not a denial of giftedness. It is evidence that the gift remains under Christ.

verses.bibleText: But if any man be ignorant, let him be ignorant.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul warns that anyone who refuses to recognize this instruction will not be recognized, or, according to another form of the wording, will remain unrecognized or ignorant. The precise phrasing varies, but the seriousness is clear.

A person cannot reject apostolic correction and still demand that the church validate his claim to spirituality. Refusal to listen has consequences. If the sense is “he is not recognized,” the church is not obligated to acknowledge the person as a trustworthy prophet or spiritual guide. If the sense is “let him remain ignorant,” Paul indicates that persistent resistance may be left to the condition it has chosen. Either way, rejection of truth exposes the claim.

This is not petty retaliation. Paul is protecting the congregation from people who use spiritual language while refusing accountability.

The warning follows an invitation to recognize the Lord’s command. Ignorance here is not the sincere lack of knowledge of someone eager to learn. It is a deliberate unwillingness to acknowledge correction. That distinction matters pastorally. Honest questions should be welcomed. A confused believer who seeks understanding is not being condemned. Paul addresses stubborn spiritual pride.

The church must not allow charisma to override character or obedience. A person may appear gifted, speak confidently, and attract followers, yet resistance to apostolic truth is a grave warning sign.

Paul’s warning encourages humility in everyone who ministers. No gift, reputation, or experience makes correction unnecessary. The proper response to apostolic instruction is not defensive self-protection but teachable obedience. Spirituality that cannot be corrected becomes dangerous. Spirituality that recognizes the Lord’s command becomes safe for the church. The seriousness of verse 38 protects the congregation from confusing impressive claims with trustworthy service.

verses.bibleText: Wherefore, brethren, covet to prophesy, and forbid not to speak with tongues.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
Paul closes without forbidding the genuine gift. Spirit-enabled human languages remain valuable for mission and may serve worship when they are understood or translated. "Do not forbid" cannot be separated from the chapter's commands: at most two or three, one at a time, with interpretation; otherwise silence. Permission is not permission for disorder, meaningless syllables, or an untestable private prayer language to dominate the assembly.

Prophecy is especially desired because it communicates directly, but every gift stands under love, Scripture, self-control, and edification. The church honors the Spirit by welcoming what He truly gives and using it in the way He directs.

verses.bibleText: Let all things be done decently and in order.

commentary.detailedExplanation:
“Let all things be done appropriately and in order.” Paul ends not with prohibition but with a comprehensive direction for everything that occurs in worship.

“Appropriately,” or decently, refers to what is fitting for a congregation belonging to Christ. Worship should reflect holiness, love, dignity, and regard for others.

“In order” means that contributions occur in a sequence that permits understanding, participation, and discernment. Tongue speakers speak one at a time with interpretation. Prophets speak in limited number and are evaluated. Speakers yield. Those causing disruption become silent. Order is not the same as rigid formality. Paul’s vision remains remarkably participatory. Hymns, teachings, revelations, tongues, interpretations, and prophecies may all contribute. Order creates the space in which such variety can serve.

Nor does order require emotional coldness. Prayer, song, conviction, gratitude, and the felt presence of God fill the chapter. The issue is whether emotion is expressed in a way that strengthens rather than overwhelms the body.

It is also peaceful. No one needs to fight for attention, prove spiritual superiority, or force acceptance of a claim. Every gift remains under Christ. The final command gathers chapters 12–14 together. The Spirit distributes diverse gifts. Love governs their use. Order enables them to build the one body.

Paul does not ask the Corinthians to choose between spiritual life and order. He calls them to Spirit-filled order—worship alive with God’s gifts and shaped by His peaceful character. In such a gathering, freedom becomes service, participation becomes fellowship, and everything moves toward the building up of Christ’s church.
